metadata.source_document: Universal Scaling Laws of Life, Cities, and Companies.pdf
metadata.description: Extracted physical parameters for Grand Unified Scaling Equation

beta.description: Scaling exponents governing power-law relationships Y ∝ N^β

biology_metabolic.source_quote: metabolic rate (B) scales with body mass (M) to the power of 3/4 (B ∝ M^{3/4})
biology_metabolic.interpretation: Sublinear scaling - efficiency increases with size, bounded growth

company_scaling.interpretation: Sublinear - emphasis on efficiency over innovation, thermodynamically destined to stop growing

company_halflife.source_quote: mortality of publicly traded companies follows an exponential decay (half-life ~10 years)

infrastructure_sublinear.source_quote: Infrastructure scales Sublinearly (N^{0.85}): Like biology, cities exhibit economies of scale. Doubling a city requires only 85% more roads, pipes, and wires
infrastructure_sublinear.interpretation: Economies of scale in physical infrastructure

socioeconomic_superlinear.source_quote: Socioeconomic Outputs scale Superlinearly (N^{1.15}): Wages, patents, crime, and GDP increase by ~15% per capita with every doubling of size
socioeconomic_superlinear.interpretation: Increasing returns to scale driven by social network topology

Fr.definition: Dimensionless quantity used for dynamic similarity in scaling models
Fr.source_quote: He introduces Froude numbers and dimensionless quantities as the tools for modeling—proving that we can predict the dynamics of a massive system (like a ship or a city) based on a small-scale model if the dimensionless ratios are preserved
Fr.application: Allows prediction of large system dynamics from small-scale models when dimensionless ratios are preserved
Fr.related_principle: Galileo's Square-Cube Law - volume (mass) increases by power of 3, while strength (cross-sectional area) increases by power of 2

C_mar.value_description: approximately one hour of travel per day
C_mar.source_quote: He also introduces Marchetti's Constant, which suggests that human mobility is constrained by a time budget (approx. one hour of travel per day), dictating the spatial spread of cities as transport technology evolves
C_mar.application: Constrains urban spatial spread as transport technology changes

T_pace.source_quote: As a city grows, the pace of life accelerates (t ∝ N^{-0.15}); people walk faster, and diseases spread more rapidly
T_pace.interpretation: Superlinear scaling forces time contraction - pace of life accelerates with city size
T_pace.validation: Validated with cell phone data showing social connectivity scales with 1.15 exponent

biological_time.description: Larger animals have slower cellular metabolic rates due to network constraints
biological_time.consequence: Larger animals accumulate entropic damage slower and live longer
biological_time.source_quote: Temperature dependence is governed by the Boltzmann factor (e^{-E/kT}), linking biological time to thermodynamics

t_sing.definition: Point where superlinear growth (N^{>1}) mathematically demands infinite energy in finite time
t_sing.source_quote: Superlinear growth (N^{>1}) mathematically results in a Finite Time Singularity, where the system demands infinite energy in a finite amount of time

R_inn.definition: Reset cycles of innovation required to avoid singularity collapse
R_inn.source_quote: To avoid collapse, the system requires 'reset' cycles of innovation. However, the theory predicts that the time interval between these innovations must accelerate (Δt → 0)

dynamics.interpretation: Accelerating treadmill requiring ever-faster innovation rates to stave off entropic collapse

magic_number_4.description: The 1/4 power appearing in biological scaling
magic_number_4.origin: Fractal networks effectively add a fourth dimension to 3D organisms, optimizing metabolic capacity

human_power_scales.source_quote: phase transition of the human species from a biological energy scale (~90 watts) to a social energy scale (~11,000 watts for a U.S. citizen)

growth_equation.description: Growth = metabolic energy input - maintenance energy
growth_equation.equilibrium_condition: dB/dt → 0 when maintenance costs equal energy supply

regime_A.name: Biology/Companies (Sublinear)
regime_A.dynamics: Bounded growth, efficiency-driven, eventually stops growing

regime_B.name: Cities (Superlinear for socioeconomic outputs)
regime_B.characteristic: β > 1 for social outputs, β < 1 for infrastructure
regime_B.dynamics: Open-ended growth, increasing returns, time contraction, singularity risk
